title: How the Squamish struck gold in Vancouver
gist: The Squamish Nation's Senakw project in Vancouver builds 6,000 rental homes on returned reserve land that is exempt from city zoning, illustrating that housing obstruction collapses when residents directly capture the upside of development. The article generalizes this into a framework: align incentives and supply a clear democratic mandate, and even a NIMBY city government will facilitate density it normally resists.

insights.claim: Housing development obstruction collapses when local residents directly capture the economic upside, as shown by Squamish members voting 87% to maximize density on their own returned land at Senakw in Vancouver.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Resident-equity models (community land trusts, estate-regen ballots) may be a more durable route to unblocking supply than top-down zoning reform; watch where landowners are made development beneficiaries.
insights.quote: Senakw shows that when local residents stand to benefit from development, obstacles to housebuilding can be overcome, to the benefit of a much broader group of people.
insights.locator: This is a story of a dispossessed group

insights.claim: When residents bear development costs directly they assign much lower value to mandates like inclusionary zoning, zero-emissions rules, parking minimums and design panels, and vote to strip them away while keeping safety standards.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Costly green/social mandates are revealed as preference-dependent, not safety-critical; jurisdictions that shed them could cut per-unit cost materially without quality loss.
insights.quote: they maintained the same safety and building standards that other Vancouver developments adhere to, but stripped away height limits, inclusionary zoning, zero-emissions requirements, parking minimums, design panels
insights.locator: It also meant opting not to impose

insights.claim: Vancouver funds nearly half its C$3.5 billion capital budget from developer payments because property taxes are kept low, creating a structural dependence on extracting land-value uplift from a few large rezonings.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Cities reliant on developer levies have a fiscal incentive favoring scarce, high-value permits over broad density, structurally suppressing total supply.
insights.quote: property taxes are low, so almost half of its C$3.5 billion capital budget comes from developer payments
insights.locator: The city is dependent on such developments

insights.claim: Vancouver planners aim to capture 75-80% of land-value uplift via case-by-case negotiated Community Amenity Contributions, an opaque process that favors concentrated towers over gentle citywide density.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Negotiated value capture caps returns to developers and structurally biases supply toward a small number of mega-projects, raising deal risk and timelines.
insights.quote: They aim to capture 75–80 percent of the land value uplift.
insights.locator: Through a mixture of fixed development fees

insights.claim: The negotiated-permit system systematically misprices projects because planners must forecast market conditions years ahead, killing viable projects when they overcharge and forgoing public revenue when they undercharge.
insights.speaker: Jens von Bergmann
insights.so_what: Discretionary value-capture introduces forecast risk that raises the cost of capital for development and disproportionately excludes small builders.
insights.quote: typically get it wrong – they either charge too much or too little

insights.claim: Vancouver downzoned systematically after a 1972 anti-development council, so that today 52% of its residential land holds just 15% of its homes.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Quantifies the locked-up land that would be available if densification rules were relaxed, sizing the latent supply for any reform thesis.
insights.quote: Today, 52 percent of Vancouver’s residential land supports just 15 percent of its homes.
insights.locator: From that point onwards, Vancouver systematically downzoned

insights.claim: Vancouver metro has an estimated 130,000-200,000 suppressed households (people doubling up who would live alone if they could afford to), a backlog equal to six to ten years of recent construction.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Sizes structural housing demand that persists regardless of cyclical rates, supporting a long-run undersupply thesis for the metro.
insights.quote: the metro area has 130,000 to 200,000 suppressed households

insights.claim: Vancouver household formation among 25-29 year-olds collapsed from two-thirds living independently in 1981 to one-third in 2021, with rental vacancy often below 1% versus ~10% in Austin.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Demonstrates demand suppression and a tight vacancy regime that supports sustained rent growth where new supply lands.
insights.quote: In 1981, two thirds of 25- to 29-year-olds in Vancouver lived in their own households; by 2021, only a third did.

insights.claim: Senakw's reserve status exempts it from Vancouver zoning, enabling 6,000 rental homes plus commercial space, equal to a full year of the city's normal housing output on one site.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the order-of-magnitude supply unlock available when a single parcel escapes discretionary zoning.
insights.quote: On just one site, Senakw will provide a year’s worth of housing normally built in the city of Vancouver.

insights.claim: Because reserve land cannot be alienated or borrowed against, Senakw required a record C$1.4 billion CMHC loan and is 100% rental on leasehold rather than for-sale condominiums.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Indigenous urban development hinges on federal financing because land-tenure rules block conventional mortgage finance; CMHC capacity is a gating factor for replication.
insights.quote: the federal government, which agreed to a C$1.4 billion loan through the Canadian Mortgage and Housing Corporation, the largest in the corporation’s history
insights.locator: These restrictions might have made the project impossible

insights.claim: The Squamish Nation's 50% stake in Senakw is expected to generate up to C$10 billion in rental income over the buildings' lifespan, roughly C$2.5 million per member across ~4,000 members.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows the scale of wealth a single upzoning event can transfer to a community holding development rights; pension fund OPTrust took the partner equity.
insights.quote: With their 50 percent stake expected to generate up to C$10 billion in rental income over the entire lifespan of the buildings
insights.locator: The financial rewards to the Squamish Nation

insights.claim: Senakw was not merely a legal loophole: both the City of Vancouver and the federal government could have blocked it by withholding services, designation or financing, but a clear community mandate and reconciliation politics induced them to cooperate.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Suggests political legitimacy, not just deregulation, is the binding constraint on density; direct resident approval can flip otherwise obstructive governments.
insights.quote: It is partly a myth that Senakw slipped through a loophole in normal land-use restrictions.
insights.locator: The second lesson concerns democratic legitimacy

insights.claim: Reconciliation political discourse, often dismissed as symbolic, had concrete effect by forcing governments to enable indigenous-led developments they would otherwise resist.
insights.speaker: Kennedy Stewart
insights.so_what: Identifies a non-economic political lever (moral/reconciliation framing) that can override entrenched NIMBY governance.
insights.quote: Promises about reconciliation forced governments’ hands when it came to enabling indigenous-led developments

insights.claim: Replicability is limited because most Canadian reserve land is non-urban, but indigenous groups can apply to convert owned land to reserve status and the federal government is currently amenable.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Flags an open pipeline of potential indigenous-led, zoning-exempt urban developments; the nearby 90-acre Jericho Lands shows the slower freehold-route alternative (~13,000 homes over 25-30 years).
insights.quote: Indigenous groups may apply to the federal government to convert land they own to reserve status, and at the moment the federal government is amenable to doing so.
insights.locator: It’s an open question how replicable
